assumptions: The declared intragroup dividend has been recognised as an intragroup monetary receivable/payable, not merely as a forecast transaction.
The entities involved are in the same consolidated group and have different functional currencies.
It is not stated whether the resulting foreign exchange gains or losses are fully eliminated on consolidation; that is treated as the key factual condition.

approaches.label: General intragroup item prohibition in consolidation
approaches.reasoning: In consolidated financial statements, hedged items generally must be with parties external to the reporting entity. Ordinary intragroup items are therefore excluded unless a specific exception applies.
approaches.practical_implication: An intragroup dividend receivable is not eligible by default in consolidation.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.5 limits hedged items in consolidated financial statements to external-party items, subject to stated exceptions.

approaches.label: Intragroup monetary item foreign currency risk hedge
approaches.reasoning: IFRS 9 specifically permits the foreign currency risk of an intragroup monetary item to be designated in consolidated financial statements when the related FX gains or losses are not fully eliminated on consolidation. The designated FX component must also be separately identifiable and reliably measurable.
conditions: The dividend receivable/payable is a recognised intragroup monetary item
The item creates FX gains or losses that are not fully eliminated on consolidation
The designated foreign currency risk component is separately identifiable and reliably measurable
The hedge relationship meets IFRS 9 documentation and effectiveness requirements
approaches.practical_implication: This is the main potentially available route for a recognised intragroup dividend receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 allows foreign currency risk of intragroup monetary items in consolidated financial statements when IAS 21-type FX differences are not fully eliminated.
Paragraphs 6.3.7 and B6.3.8 require a designated risk component to be separately identifiable and reliably measurable; paragraph 6.4.1 adds documentation and effectiveness criteria.
IFRIC 16 addresses a different hedge model (net investment hedges), so it does not displace the specific IFRS 9 exception for intragroup monetary items.

approaches.label: Forecast intragroup transaction exception
approaches.reasoning: A separate IFRS 9 exception exists for highly probable forecast intragroup transactions whose foreign currency risk affects consolidated profit or loss. That exception is aimed at forecast items, not a dividend already recognised as a receivable.
approaches.practical_implication: Do not rely on the forecast-transaction exception once the dividend has become a recognised receivable.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraph 6.3.6 and paragraphs B6.3.5-B6.3.6 permit certain highly probable forecast intragroup transactions if denominated in another currency and if the FX risk affects consolidated profit or loss.

approaches.label: Net investment hedge route
approaches.reasoning: IFRIC 16 applies only to hedges of net investments in foreign operations and says it should not be applied by analogy to other hedge accounting types. A recognised intragroup dividend receivable is not presented in the context as a net investment hedge item.
approaches.practical_implication: Net investment hedge guidance should not be used as the basis for this receivable unless the exposure is genuinely a qualifying net investment amount.

source_contributions.summary: Paragraphs 7-8 limit the interpretation to net investment hedges and prohibit applying it by analogy to other hedge accounting types.
Paragraphs 2-3 and 10 frame the hedged item as an amount of net assets of a foreign operation and the hedged risk as FX differences between the parent and foreign operation functional currencies.
Paragraph 6.5.2 identifies a hedge of a net investment in a foreign operation as a separate hedge type from hedges of recognised items or forecast transactions.
